titleStmt.title: The Lone Woman and Last Indians Digital Archive

respStmt.orgName: University of South Carolina Provost Humanities Grants
University of South Carolina Department of English
Center for Digital Humanities at the University of South Carolina
Magellan Scholar Program at the University of South Carolina
National Society of the Daughters of the American Revolution
Carnegie-Whitney Grant from the American Library Association

availability.p: Periodicals and books published before 1 January 1923 are in the public domain. If re-publishing, quoting from, or reproducing images from this digital archive, we request that you cite it as follows: Lone Woman and Last Indians Digital Archive, Center for Digital Humanities, University of South Carolina.

Periodicals and books published on or after 1 January 1923 may be protected by U.S. Copyright Law. All attempts to secure permission for republishing post-1923 material in this archive have been made. The names, logos, and identifying images, as well as the content of the articles, remain the property of the periodicals and/or of the authors and artists themselves. Further reproduction, as well as quotation beyond fair use, is prohibited; permission must be obtained from the copyright holder. Archival material published in this archive is used with permission of the institution or library that holds it. Further reproduction, as well as quotation beyond fair use, is prohibited; permission must be obtained from the copyright holder.
Responsibility for any use of the contents of this digital archive rests exclusively with the user.

div.p: A party of men engaged in craw-fishing on San Nicolas island relate to the San Diego Union a strange tale of earthquakes, winds, storms and meteorological disturbances which occurred there about the first of July. Coral harbor, the only little harbor possessed by the island, was completely filled up with sand during these disturbances. Speaking of these islands, the San Diego Union says:

"San Nicolas is the most westerly of the group known as the Santa Barbara islands, and is about 150 miles distant from San Diego. Twenty-five years ago it was covered with grass, which afforded pasture for thousands of sheep, and its owners lived there in a fine residence.

"A strange transformation has been going on since then. It is now a windy, barren, sandy waste, where but a few hundred sheep, without a shepherd, eke out a miserable existence. It is but eight miles long and four wide, but wherever one digs can be found human bones and skulls. In the distant past it was either densely populated, or a burial ground, or a great battlefield.

["]Some fifty years ago the last inhabitant was found in the person of an old woman. A Santa Barbara otter hunter named Nidever found her, and took her to his home, where she died shortly after, without being able to make any one understand the language. She was found in a cave, subsisting on fish which she caught with hooks made out of abalone shells. Her discovery caused considerable interest in the scientific world at the time.
"Captain Mullet, who had visited the islands many times during the past twenty years, says he was glad to get away from it with his men, and has visited it for the last time."
